title: Forwarding One Pager
content:
# Brayan Tenesaca
**AI Consultant for Catholic Business Leaders**

brayan@nextgenerationlearners.com | 203-460-4183

---

## The Problem

Catholic CEOs and founders are serious about their vocation to marriage and fatherhood. But their companies are stealing their time. Repetitive tasks, inbox overload, manual workflows: these are keeping men at their desks when they should be at dinner, at Mass, and with their families.

This is not a discipline problem. It is a systems problem.

---

## What I Do

I come into a Catholic business leader's operation, map exactly where his time is going, and build the AI systems that eliminate the work keeping him from his family and his faith.

The result: measurable hours returned every week, reinvested into the vocation God called him to.

---

## The Vocation Audit

A focused engagement where I identify exactly where AI can give you your time back.

**What you get:**
- 90-minute discovery session
- Prioritized list of 3-5 AI systems that recover measurable hours per week
- Estimated time and cost savings for Year 1
- A clear proposal if you want to move forward

**Investment: $2,000**

---

## Who I Am

I am 18 years old, a Catholic, and a freshman at Babson College. I co-founded Next Generation Learners and have launched AI programs from concept to execution in under three weeks.

I started building this work after realizing that the technology I was creating was pulling me away from what mattered. I walked away from a working business because it was not serving God. Now I use my technical skills to go to war for Catholic fathers and CEOs.

I am backed by Chauncey St. John (Hallow) and Gary Sheng (Applied AI Society, Forbes 30 Under 30, ex-Google).

---

## Get in Touch

If this resonates, reach out directly. I am ready to work.

**Brayan Tenesaca**
brayan@nextgenerationlearners.com
203-460-4183
[brayantenesaca.com]

*"Whatever you do, work at it with all your heart, as working for the Lord, not for human masters." -- Colossians 3:23*

title: Strategic Blocker Plan
content:
# Battle Plan: First Legatus Client by April 14

*Created: 2026-03-31 | Deadline: April 14, 2026 — signed deal before Gary meeting*

## The Blocker in One Sentence

You have the credential, the mission, and the skills — but you are still operating inside the old model instead of fully stepping into the new one.

## The Insight

The library work is not the business. It is the proof. You need to use what you already have to get in the room with one Legatus member, deliver a transformation, and let the network do the rest. Legatus is a referral culture. One real win compounds.

**The goal by April 14:** Walk into the Gary Sheng meeting with a signed deal — or at minimum a discovery conversation completed and a proposal out. Show Gary the blade is sharp.

---

## The Offer (Finalized)

### Name: The Vocation Audit

**One-sentence pitch:**
*"I help Catholic business leaders identify exactly where AI can give them their time back — so they can be present for their families, their faith, and their mission."*

**Value frame — growth investment, not expense:**
The goal is never to be seen as another vendor or cost line. You are a growth investment. The framing: "I show you exactly where time is being lost, quantify what that costs you, and build systems that give it back — so you can invest that time in your family, your faith, and growing your company." Every dollar spent with you is returned 10x in recovered time and focused energy. Position this in every conversation.

**What the Vocation Audit actually is:**
The 90-minute session is the destination — not the first ask. You earn the right to that session through the email sequence below. The audit itself is:
- A focused conversation where you map how they spend their time, where the friction is, what keeps them at the desk
- An audit document: prioritized list of 3–5 AI systems that would give measurable hours back each week
- Estimated hours recovered per week and per year
- A clear proposal for implementation if they want to move forward

**What you are selling:** Not technology. Not a session. Not a document. Time with their family. The ability to be present at dinner. Frame every touchpoint — email, call, proposal — around that.

**What makes you different:** You are 18, Catholic, and you walked away from a working business because it was pulling you away from what mattered. No one else in this space has that story. Lead with it.

### Pricing (Applied AI Society framework)

| Engagement | Price | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Vocation Audit | **$2,000** | Discovery minimum — do not go below this |
| 1-Month Implementation Sprint | **$5,000** | One problem, one deliverable |
| 3–6 Month Transformation | **$15,000+** | Multi-system, deep engagement |
| Monthly Retainer | **$2,000–$3,000/mo** | Ongoing OS management + optimization |

**How to price any specific engagement:**
1. Ask: "How many hours a week does this cost you?" Multiply by their loaded hourly rate (~$200–$500/hr for a CEO)
2. Reflect it back: "This costs you roughly $X per year."
3. Charge 10–20% of Year 1 value. They keep 80–90%.
4. Say: "I solve this for $Y — which is Z% of what it's costing you annually."

**Rule:** Price by the transformation, not the hour. Never quote an hourly rate.
**Close rate diagnostic:** If you are closing 70%+ of conversations, you are undercharging. Some no's are proof you are priced correctly.

---

## The Outreach System

### The Email Sequence Strategy (Learned from NGL)

**Key lesson from NGL library outreach:** High open rates but people hesitated to commit to a call immediately. The fix: do not ask for a call in the first email. Get them replying first. Once they are replying, they are emotionally invested. Then ask for the call on email 2 or 3.

**The pipeline from cold email to signed deal:**

```
Email 1: Mission announcement → goal is ONE reply
Email 2: Deepen the conversation, share something valuable → goal is continued dialogue
Email 3: Ask for a 15-minute call
Call: Listen for pain, diagnose the problem
Post-call: Send the Vocation Audit proposal ($2,000)
Signed deal
```

**Email 1 — Mission Announcement (goal: one reply)**

Structure (under 150 words):
1. Identity: "I am an 18-year-old Catholic Babson student and founder of Next Generation Learners."
2. Specific observation about them — researched, personal, not generic flattery
3. Mission statement: faith-first, one sentence
4. A question about THEIR pain or experience — not an ask for time. Something they can answer in one sentence: *"Is this something you see in the men around you?"* or *"What do you see as the biggest operational burden Catholic CEOs carry?"*
5. Hunger closer: "I am a hungry 18-year-old and will make you proud."
6. Sign off: "In Christ, Brayan Tenesaca"

No pitch. No ask for a call. Just open a door.

**Email 2 — Deepen (goal: keep the dialogue going)**

- Open by acknowledging what they said specifically
- Add one layer: a specific observation, a short story, something that makes them think
- End with another easy question or reflect their pain back to them
- Still no ask for time — let the investment build

**Email 3 — The Ask (goal: book 15 minutes)**

- Reference the conversation: "Based on what you shared..."
- Make the ask simply: *"I would love 15 minutes to hear more about [specific thing they mentioned]. Would that work?"*
- Short. The relationship is already warm. No re-explaining needed.

**Proof this works:** The Chauncey sequence followed this exact pattern. Email 1 ended with a soft ask ("if you are open to a conversation") — Chauncey replied asking what Brayan hoped to learn. Email 2 gave a specific, substantive answer. Chauncey sent his Calendly. Three emails to a booked call with a Forbes-backed Catholic operator.

All emails drafted by Jarvis in Brayan's voice, approved by Brayan before sending. See `user/voice-profile.md` for full voice guidelines.

### Cold Outreach — The Right Frame

You are not cold-pitching a service. You are announcing a mission and finding who is already aligned with it. That is how you met Chauncey. Keep doing it. The Applied AI docs say warm networks close deals — they are right. Cold outreach gets you into warm conversations. Once someone replies, the relationship has started.

**The rule:** Cold email gets a reply. Replies build investment. Investment leads to a call. The call closes the deal. Never try to close in the email.

### Outreach Queue System

All outreach is drafted here and approved by Brayan before sending. Brayan reviews, edits, approves, then sends.

**Active outreach log:**

| Person | Role | Status | Next Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Father David | Bridgeport Diocese priest | Awaiting response | Follow up Day 7 if no reply |
| Stephen Henley | Legatus President | Cold email sent ✅ | Awaiting response |
| Legatus board members | Board | Cold email sent ✅ | Awaiting response |
| Chauncey St John | Hallow, backer | Replied + Brayan replied ✅ — told him Northeast Catholic CEOs, Legatus members, chapter president or chaplain as starting point | Awaiting his connections |
| Carlos Aspillaga | Mentor | Call booked ✅ April 2 at 4:30pm | Make the specific ask on call |
| Frank Rowella | Mentor | Email chain warm ✅ | Waiting for his reply before next message |
| Chris Pannone | Mentor | Email sent ✅ | Awaiting response |
| Ethan Hale | Advisor, $20-50M co. | Not yet contacted | Text drafted — casual, he is busy buying a bank |
| Gary Sheng | Backer, Applied AI Society | Message sent ✅ | Awaiting response |
| Steven Ingram | Legatus VP Northeast Region | Email sent ✅ subject: "you would be interested" | Awaiting response |
| Laura Sacha | Legatus COO | Email sent ✅ subject: "thank you for your work" | Awaiting response |
| John Hunt | Legatus Executive Director | Not yet contacted | Appointed Nov 2025 — draft next |

**The specific ask to make in every warm conversation:**
*"Do you know any Catholic business leaders — specifically anyone in Legatus — I should be talking to?"*

Until someone hears that sentence, they cannot help you even if they want to.

### The Legatus Staff Strategy

Legatus national staff (executive directors, program managers, chapter coordinators) serve the same mission you do. They are gatekeepers and advocates who can introduce you to chapter priests, presidents, and members.

Find them: Legatus national website staff directory + RocketReach.
Use the mission announcement template above, personalized to their role.

---

## 14-Day Action Plan

### Days 1–3: Lock the Offer
- [ ] Finalize the one-page Vocation Audit document (being built today)
- [ ] Practice the one-sentence pitch out loud until it sounds natural
- [ ] Do NOT build a website. Do NOT make a deck. One page only.

### Days 4–7: Activate All Warm Paths
- [ ] Carlos call — make the specific ask: "Do you know any Catholic business leaders or Legatus members I should talk to?"
- [ ] Frank Rowella — send next message leaning into his family comment, then make the ask
- [ ] Ethan Hale — draft ask email (Jarvis will write, Brayan approves)
- [ ] Follow up on Chauncey if no response by Day 5
- [ ] Follow up on Legatus chapter outreach if no response by Day 7

### Days 8–11: Legatus Staff Outreach
- [ ] Find 3–5 Legatus national staff via website + RocketReach
- [ ] Jarvis drafts each email, Brayan approves and sends
- [ ] Goal: 1 discovery conversation booked with a Legatus-connected person

### Days 12–13: Close
- [ ] Run discovery session if booked
- [ ] Send Vocation Audit proposal
- [ ] Follow up on any open proposals — push for signature

### Day 14: Gary Meeting (April 14)
Walk in with:
1. The signed deal — or a proposal out with a verbal yes
2. The one-page Vocation Audit document
3. A one-paragraph summary of outreach run and conversations had
4. A specific ask for Gary: *"Do you know any Catholic business leaders or Legatus members I should be talking to? I am ready to run a discovery session."*

---

## Phase 2 — Compound (After April 14)

**Goal:** Use the first win to get the next three.

1. Document the transformation story with the client's permission
2. Ask for one referral inside the Legatus chapter
3. Present at a chapter meeting: "How Catholic business leaders are using AI to protect their time for family and faith." Teaching, not selling. Clients come to you.
4. Cap initial clients at 3–5 retained relationships. Go deep before wide.

---

## The Black Swans to Watch

1. **NGL distraction.** Honor committed programs. Sign no new library contracts.
2. **Underpricing.** $2,000 minimum on the audit. The price signals seriousness.
3. **Building before validating.** Sell first. Build second. No tech until someone pays.
4. **Closing in the email.** Cold emails get conversations. Conversations close deals.
5. **Going alone.** Loop in Ryan and Anselm on the pivot.

---

## Reference

Applied AI Society practitioner docs: `https://docs.appliedaisociety.org/docs/playbooks/practitioner`
Pull Pricing, Finding Clients, and Pilot Pitch before every client conversation.

---

## The Reminder When You Feel Behind

You are 18. You have already built and launched a real business, gotten credible backers, and identified a mission most people spend their entire lives searching for. The fear that you are not enough is the enemy. The antidote is action. One conversation. One client. One win. Then the next.

*"Be strong and courageous. Do not be afraid; do not be discouraged, for the Lord your God will be with you wherever you go."* — Joshua 1:9

title: Vocation Audit One Pager
content:
# The Vocation Audit

*Brayan Tenesaca — Next Generation Learners*

---

## The Problem

You built a company that serves God, your family, and your mission.

But somewhere along the way, the work started owning you. The inbox, the reports, the repetitive tasks — they stack up. You are at your desk when you should be at dinner. You are on your phone when your children are in the room. The work is important, but it is stealing the hours that belong to the people who matter most.

This is not a time management problem. It is a systems problem. And it is solvable.

---

## Who This Is For

Catholic business leaders — CEOs, founders, executives — who are serious about their vocation to marriage and fatherhood and refuse to let their company cost them their family.

---

## What the Vocation Audit Is

A focused 90-minute session where we map exactly where your time is going, identify what AI can eliminate, and show you what your week looks like when those hours are returned to you.

**What you walk away with:**

- A clear picture of where your time is being stolen
- A prioritized list of 3–5 AI systems that would give you measurable hours back each week
- An estimate of hours recovered per week and per year
- A clear proposal if you want to move forward with implementation

**What this is not:** A generic AI consultation. A slide deck. A strategy document you will never read. This is a working session with a direct output.

---

## The Investment

**$2,000**

To put that in context: if this recovers 5 hours a week for a CEO whose time is worth $300/hour, that is $78,000 returned to you in Year 1. The audit costs 2.5% of that.

---

## Who I Am

My name is Brayan Tenesaca. I am 18 years old, a Catholic, and a freshman at Babson College. I co-founded Next Generation Learners and have built and launched AI programs from the ground up — the first one went from idea to execution in 3 weeks for $240.

I started pivoting toward this work after I realized that the technology I was building was pulling me away from what mattered. I walked away from a working business because it wasn't serving God. Now I use my technical skills to go to war for Catholic fathers and CEOs — men who are drowning in digital noise when they should be with their wives and children.

I am backed by Chauncey St John (Hallow) and Gary Sheng (Applied AI Society, Forbes 30 Under 30).

---

## What Happens Next

We schedule a 90-minute session. I come in, ask the right questions, and map your operation. You walk out with a clear document and a decision to make. No pressure. No pitch.

If you want to move forward with implementation, we scope it together and price it based on the value it creates — not the hours it takes.

---

*"Whatever you do, work at it with all your heart, as working for the Lord, not for human masters."* — Colossians 3:23

---

**Brayan Tenesaca**
Co-Founder, Next Generation Learners
[contact info]

title: North Star Value
content:
# The North Star: What You're Actually Selling

## It's Not Time. It's Presence.

Roche is a superintendent doing the job of a superintendent AND a principal. He's drowning in admin — meetings, minutes, agendas, emails, board prep, parent communication. Every hour he spends on admin is an hour he's NOT spending on:

- Walking into a classroom and watching a teacher teach
- Having a conversation with a struggling student
- Thinking strategically about his district's future
- Being present at his son's soccer game
- Sitting at dinner with his family without his inbox on his mind

**You're not selling time savings. You're selling presence.**

When Roche gets 5-10 hours back per week, those hours don't disappear. They become something. The question is: what do they become?

For a business owner, saved time becomes revenue. That's the Applied AI framework — and it works for businesses.

For a superintendent, saved time becomes **the ability to be the leader he became a superintendent to be.** He didn't go into education to draft meeting minutes. He went into education because he cares about kids. Every hour of admin is an hour stolen from that mission.

**Your Jarvis gives him back the hours that matter.**

---

## The 4 Layers of Value (Education Leader Edition)

### Layer 1: Time Recovered (Quantifiable)
Hours per week saved on admin tasks. This is real and measurable. But it's the floor, not the ceiling.

| Task | Current Hours/Week | With Jarvis | Saved |
|------|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| Meeting minutes + action items | 3-4 hrs | 0.5 hrs | ~3 hrs |
| Drafting email responses | 3-5 hrs | 0.5-1 hr | ~3 hrs |
| Building weekly agendas | 1-2 hrs | 0.25 hrs | ~1.5 hrs |
| Board meeting prep | 2-3 hrs | 0.5 hrs | ~2 hrs |
| Parent/community communication | 2-3 hrs | 0.5 hrs | ~2 hrs |
| **Total** | **11-17 hrs** | **2-3 hrs** | **~10 hrs** |

At Roche's loaded rate (~$75-85/hr for a superintendent), that's $39,000-44,000/year in recovered time.

But that number alone doesn't capture it. Those 10 hours become something.

### Layer 2: Capability Gained (What Was Impossible Before)
Things Roche literally cannot do today, no matter how many hours he works:

- **Institutional memory that doesn't live in his head.** Every meeting, every decision, every conversation — captured, searchable, connected. When a board member asks "didn't we discuss this last October?" he has the answer in seconds.
- **Personalized communication at scale.** Draft 30 unique parent emails about 30 different students, each referencing specific context. Impossible by hand. Trivial with Jarvis.
- **Pattern recognition across months of meetings.** "What topics keep coming up that we haven't resolved?" AI can surface this. A human brain can't hold 6 months of meeting notes.
- **Decision support with full context.** "Before I respond to this email, what do I know about this person? What have we discussed before? What's the background?" Jarvis knows.

This layer is where the "holy shit" moment lives. It's not faster — it was impossible.

### Layer 3: Leadership Quality (The Real Transformation)
When admin work shrinks and capabilities expand, something changes in how Roche leads:

- **He responds to every email thoughtfully** instead of triaging and dropping balls
- **He follows up on every action item** instead of things falling through cracks
- **He walks into board meetings fully prepared** instead of scrambling the night before
- **He has time to visit classrooms** instead of being chained to his desk
- **He makes better decisions** because he has context at his fingertips instead of relying on memory

This isn't automation. This is a superintendent becoming a better superintendent.

### Layer 4: Legacy and Influence (The Long Game)
- He becomes the superintendent who figured out AI — not for students, for HIMSELF
- When he convenes that group of superintendents, he has something to show
- His district becomes the model others learn from
- His story becomes: "I was drowning in admin. Now I spend my time where it matters. Here's how."

**This is the story that sells your next 10 clients. Not a case study. His lived experience.**

---

## The North Star (Memorize This)

**"I give education leaders back the hours that matter — and the capabilities they didn't know they could have — so they can lead instead of manage."**

That's it. That's what you're selling. Not automation. Not prompts. Not tools. You're selling:
- Hours back → to be present (in classrooms, at home, with students)
- Capabilities gained → to do things that were impossible before
- Leadership quality → to be the leader they became an educator to be

When you sit with Roche and show him the Jarvis, that's the frame. Not "this saves you time on meeting minutes." Instead: "This gives you back the hours you're supposed to spend in classrooms and with students. And it gives you capabilities you've never had."

---

## How to Use This in the Show-and-Tell

When you interview Roche (Stage 3 of the pipeline), ask these questions:

1. "Walk me through a typical week. Where does your time go?"
2. "If you could get 10 hours back this week, what would you do with them?"
3. "What's the thing you WISH you had time for but never do?"
4. "When was the last time you visited a classroom just to watch a teacher teach?"
5. "What falls through the cracks that you hate dropping?"

His answers tell you exactly which layer of value matters most to HIM. Maybe it's presence with students. Maybe it's board prep confidence. Maybe it's just not feeling behind on email every single day.

Whatever he says — that becomes the value you reference in the proposal. Not your words. His.

title: Pricing Deep Dive
content:
# Pricing Deep Dive: The Real Math for Education Leaders

## Roche's World

**CT superintendent average salary:** $171,157/year
**Small K-8 district (Woodstock):** Likely $130,000-150,000 range (25th percentile = $151K, but Woodstock is tiny — ~400 students, rural northeast CT)
**Loaded hourly rate:** ~$75-85/hr (salary + benefits ÷ ~1,800 working hours)

**Key context:** This is a public employee. He doesn't have a P&L. He doesn't think in ROI. His budget is taxpayer money approved by a school board. Every dollar is scrutinized. He can't swipe a corporate card — he likely needs board approval for anything significant.

---

## Why the 10% Framework Doesn't Fit Here

The Applied AI playbook says: charge 10-20% of Year 1 value. For Roche, the math would be:

- 10 hours/week saved × $80/hr × 52 weeks = ~$41,600/year in recovered time
- 10-20% of that = $4,160-$8,320

**The problem:** Roche doesn't have $4,000-8,000 in discretionary budget for a personal AI tool. This isn't a business investing in growth. This is a public servant managing a small district's budget. Asking for $5,000 feels like asking him to justify a line item to the board of education for "AI for the superintendent."

**Your instinct is right.** The 10% model is built for business owners who can see the ROI in their P&L. Roche sees value differently.

**But also: don't underprice.** The playbook is also right that pricing too low signals "this isn't serious." Free means he won't use it. $500 means it's a hobby project. The price has to say: "this is a professional engagement that will change how you operate."

---

## How Roche Actually Makes Buying Decisions

Think about what he ALREADY spends money on without blinking:

| What | Cost | Why He Says Yes |
|------|------|-----------------|
| Bringing Kristy + Gina from Danbury for PD day | Travel + substitute costs, probably $1,000-2,000 | It makes his teachers better |
| Professional development budget (annual) | $5,000-15,000 for a small district | State requirement + teacher growth |
| Technology tools (Google Workspace, SIS, etc.) | $3,000-10,000/year | Infrastructure he can't operate without |
| Substitute teachers when he's doing superintendent work | $150-200/day | He literally can't be in two places at once |

**The insight:** He doesn't buy "time savings." He buys things that make his district better, his teachers stronger, or his operations possible. The tool has to fit into a category he already understands and already has budget for.

---

## The Three Ways to Price This

### Option A: Professional Development Framing
**What it sounds like:** "AI leadership training for the superintendent"
**Price range:** $1,500-3,000
**Why this works:** Every district has a PD budget. This fits into an existing line item. No board approval for a new category — it's professional development.

**What's included:**
- Setup of the Jarvis system (installation, configuration)
- 2 in-person training sessions (2 hours each)
- Custom skills built for his workflow (meeting minutes, agendas, email drafts)
- 30-day support window for questions

**How he justifies it:** "I invested in AI professional development for district leadership."

### Option B: Consulting Engagement
**What it sounds like:** "AI systems consulting for district operations"
**Price range:** $3,000-5,000
**Why this works:** Districts hire consultants regularly — curriculum consultants, technology consultants, facilities consultants. This is an AI operations consultant.

**What's included:**
- Everything in Option A
- Full workflow audit (interview + documentation)
- Custom tool development (calendar, CRM, board prep system)
- 60-day support window
- One "new tool build" per month for 3 months

**How he justifies it:** "We brought in an AI consultant to optimize district operations."

### Option C: The Pilot-to-Retainer (Long Game)
**What it sounds like:** "Let me build you a pilot. If it works, we talk about ongoing."
**Pilot price:** $2,000-2,500
**Retainer price:** $500-1,000/month after

**Why this works:** Low entry point. He says yes easily. After 2 weeks, he's dependent on it. The retainer is justified by the ongoing value — new tools, updates, priority support. $500-1,000/month from a district budget is invisible.

**How he justifies it:** Pilot comes from PD or consulting budget. Retainer comes from technology or operations budget.

---

## What's Actually Right for Roche (My Recommendation)

**Start with Option A: $2,000-2,500.** Here's why:

1. **It's your first paid engagement.** The playbook says: "Start with a lower price to get one case study. Then double it on the next engagement." Roche is your case study, not your biggest payday.

2. **It fits his budget.** $2,000-2,500 fits comfortably in a PD or professional services line item. He can probably approve this without going to the board.

3. **It's not free.** He has skin in the game. He'll actually USE it because he paid for it. The playbook is explicit: "Clients who won't pay a reasonable amount for a 2-week pilot are not clients you want."

4. **The real money is downstream.** When Roche shows this to 5 other superintendents and they each want one, you charge $3,500-5,000 because you have a case study. When Zaleta wants a district-wide implementation, you charge $10,000-15,000 because you have proof.

5. **It buys you the story.** "I built a personal AI system for a Connecticut superintendent. Here's what happened." That story is worth more than the $2,500 difference between Option A and Option B.

---

## The Value Beyond Price (What You Need to Internalize)

You asked: "What is the value? It can't just be saving him time."

You're right. Here's the full stack of value, and this is what you need to believe in your bones before you ever quote a price:

### You Are Giving Him Something He Can't Get Anywhere Else

1. **No consultant will build him a personal Jarvis.** McKinsey won't do this. Deloitte won't do this. The IT department won't do this. You are the only person offering to build a personal AI operating system for a superintendent. There is no market comparison. That means YOU set the price.

2. **No app does what this does.** Granola does meeting notes. Gmail does email. Google Calendar does scheduling. None of them CONNECT to each other with context about his people, his decisions, his district. The Jarvis is the connective tissue between all of it.

3. **No amount of YouTube tutorials will get him here.** He could watch 100 hours of "AI for educators" content and he still wouldn't have what you're building him. Because you're not teaching him a tool — you're building a system around HIS specific workflow.

4. **You are 18 years old and you built this for yourself.** That is the most powerful proof possible. You're not selling theory. You're selling a system you use every single day. When he sees your Jarvis working — your CRM, your calendar, your email drafting, your outreach queue — he's seeing a living, breathing demo. No other consultant can do that.

### The Value Equation (Simple Version)

**What he gets:**
- 10+ hours/week back (quantifiable)
- Capabilities he's never had (institutional memory, personalized communication at scale, pattern recognition)
- Better leadership (more classroom visits, better decisions, nothing falling through cracks)
- A story to tell other superintendents

**What it costs him:**
- $2,000-2,500 (less than one month of a part-time secretary)
- 4 hours of his time (two training sessions)

**What it costs him to NOT do this:**
- He keeps spending 15+ hours/week on admin
- He keeps being stretched between superintendent and principal duties
- He keeps dropping balls on follow-ups
- He keeps showing up to the superintendent meeting with nothing to show on AI
- His teachers keep hearing about AI without seeing their leader use it

---

## The Pricing Conversation (When It Happens)

Don't bring up pricing at the PD event. Don't bring it up at the show-and-tell. Let HIM ask. If he doesn't ask, you bring it up in the proposal email — and by then, he's already seen the value.

When the moment comes, say:

> "Based on what you showed me about your workflow, the setup and training would be [price]. That covers the system, the custom skills for your specific tasks, two hands-on training sessions, and 30 days of support. If it doesn't change how you work, we'll make it right."

**Then stop talking.** The playbook says the worst thing you can do is fill the silence after quoting a price. Let him process. Let him respond.

If he says "that's a lot," say: "I understand. What would feel right for you?" Then negotiate from there. The floor is $1,500. Below that, it's not a professional engagement — it's a favor.

If he says "I need to check the budget," say: "Absolutely. Take your time. I'll send everything in writing so you have it when you're ready."

If he says "yes," say: "Great. Let's pick a start date."

---

## The Pricing Ladder (Your Next 6 Months)

| Client # | Who | Price | Why |
|-----------|-----|-------|-----|
| 1 | Roche | $2,000-2,500 | First case study. Prove the model. |
| 2-3 | Roche's superintendent friends | $3,500-4,000 | You have a case study now. Price goes up. |
| 4-5 | Zaleta/Danbury (district pilot) | $5,000-8,000 | District-level engagement. More scope, more value. |
| 6-10 | Referrals from the network | $5,000+ | You're established. The skills library is built. Setup is faster. |

**The playbook says:** "Keep doubling until you start hearing no." Client #1 at $2,500. Client #3 at $4,000. Client #5 at $7,000. That's how you find your real price.

title: Email Ai Chrome Extension Spec
content:
# Product Spec: Forge Email AI (Chrome Extension)

## The Problem

Roche gets 500 emails/day. Brayan spends 2-3 hours on email. The current workflow is:
1. Open Gmail
2. Read email manually
3. Think about what to say
4. Draft reply manually (or paste into ChatGPT, get draft, paste back)
5. Edit, send
6. Repeat 50-100 times

This is broken. The AI lives in one tab, email lives in another, and context lives in Jarvis on the computer. None of them talk to each other.

## The Vision

A Chrome extension that sits inside Gmail and connects to Jarvis. When you open an email:

1. **Auto-categorize** — Urgent / Action Needed / FYI / Noise (based on keywords, sender, content)
2. **Context pull** — Cross-reference sender against Jarvis people files. Show: who is this person, last interaction, relationship context
3. **Auto-draft** — Generate a reply in your voice/tone, referencing relevant context from Jarvis
4. **One-click send** — Review, edit if needed, send without leaving Gmail

## How It Connects to Jarvis

The extension needs to read from the Jarvis file system. Two approaches:

### Option A: Local Server (Sovereign)
- A lightweight local server runs on the user's computer
- Extension communicates with localhost
- Server reads Jarvis markdown files (people/, artifacts/, etc.)
- Fully sovereign — nothing leaves the machine
- Requires: user has Jarvis set up locally

### Option B: Forge Server Bridge
- Extension communicates with the Forge server (where CRM/Calendar already live)
- Server has access to people data, interaction history
- Less sovereign but easier setup for clients
- Could work as the "quick start" version for Roche

### Recommended: Start with Option B, offer Option A as premium

## Core Features (MVP)

### 1. Email Categorization
When Gmail inbox loads, extension scans visible emails and adds colored tags:
- 🔴 **Urgent** — keywords: "urgent", "ASAP", "deadline", sender is flagged as priority
- 🟡 **Action** — requires a response or decision
- 🔵 **FYI** — informational, no response needed
- ⚫ **Noise** — newsletters, ads, bulk email

### 2. Sender Context Card
Click extension icon on any email → sidebar shows:
- Person's name, role, organization (from Jarvis people files)
- Last interaction date and summary
- Relationship notes
- "No profile found" → option to create one

### 3. Smart Draft
Button inside Gmail compose: "Draft with Forge"
- Reads the email thread
- Pulls sender context from Jarvis
- Generates reply in the user's voice (trained on past sent emails)
- Inserts draft into compose window
- User reviews, edits, sends

### 4. Tinder-Style Triage (from existing outreach tool)
Quick swipe/button interface for processing email:
- ✅ Approve draft & send
- ✏️ Edit before sending
- 📌 Flag for later
- 🗑️ Archive/dismiss

## Technical Stack

- Chrome Extension (Manifest V3)
- Gmail API or DOM injection for reading emails
- Backend: Forge server (Node.js, already exists)
- AI: Claude API for draft generation
- Context: Read from Jarvis files or Forge database

## Pricing Implications

This is the recurring revenue play. The Jarvis sprint ($2,500-5,000) is the setup. The Email AI extension is the monthly retainer:
- $200-500/month for ongoing email AI + context updates
- Justified: saves 10-15 hours/week of email time
- At $75/hr superintendent rate = $3,000-4,500/month in value
- Charging $200-500 is 5-10% of monthly value

## Build Priority

**Do NOT build this before closing Roche.** This is what you build DURING or AFTER the sprint. It's the "continued development" tier on the proposal page. Sell the sprint first, deliver value, then introduce this as the next level.

## Timeline Estimate

- MVP (categorization + draft button): 2-3 days of focused building
- Context integration with Jarvis: 1-2 additional days
- Polish + testing: 1 day
- Total: ~1 week for a working MVP

This becomes a demo-able feature during the Roche sprint if time allows.

title: Irrational Fears Vs Reality
content:
# Irrational Fears vs. Reality

*Written April 4, 2026. Reference this when you feel the fear creeping in before a proposal, a pitch, or a close.*

---

## Fear #1: "I'll scare him off with the price"

### The Fear
$2,500 feels like a lot of money. What if Roche sees the number and thinks I'm just a kid trying to charge too much?

### The Reality

**You're charging 3.2% of Year 1 value.**

The Applied AI pricing playbook says: *"Price at 10-20% of Year 1 business impact value."* ([Applied AI — Pricing](https://docs.appliedaisociety.org/docs/playbooks/practitioner/pricing))

Roche's problem costs him ~$79,000/year in lost time. At $2,500, you're charging 3.2%. You'd have to charge $7,900 to even hit the FLOOR of the recommended range.

The playbook also names this exact fear: **"Selling out of your own wallet."** The signs are:
- Embarrassment quoting your price
- Preemptive discounting
- Thinking the fee is expensive
- Comparing yourself to lower-earning peers

*"The fix: Price based on client outcomes (revenue unlocked, hours recovered, risk mitigated), not your effort."*

**Holy shit moment:** The Breakthrough Coach charges $2,000-3,500 per person for a group workshop with a paper binder. You're charging $2,500 for a custom-built sovereign operating system that lives on his computer forever, with 30 days of direct support. He's getting MORE for LESS.

**Holy shit moment #2:** Roche makes ~$160,000/year. $2,500 is 1.6% of his salary. He probably spends more than that on professional development conferences annually. This isn't a financial stretch for him. This is a rounding error on his professional learning budget.

---

## Fear #2: "I can't deliver enough value"

### The Fear
What if I set up Jarvis and he doesn't use it? What if it doesn't actually save him time? What if he thinks it was a waste?

### The Reality

**You already delivered value for free and he couldn't believe it.**

On April 3, you:
- Built a financial literacy app in 5 minutes in front of teachers
- Deployed a multiplication game on a live website in real-time
- Created a QR code email capture system while someone else was presenting
- Updated your website live during the session
- Showed him your personal time tracking, email outreach, calendar system

His reaction: *"That's amazing."* His assistant principal told Gina: *"He's a real person."* Teachers said: *"That's crazy."*

You did all of that for FREE. Now imagine what you deliver when you have a full day dedicated to his specific problems.

**Holy shit moment:** The MVP Jarvis docs say setup takes 3.5-4 hours even with technical experience. You have MORE than technical experience — you built your own Jarvis and run your entire business on it. You can set up his in half that time and spend the rest of the day training him and customizing. ([Applied AI — Minimum Viable Jarvis](https://docs.appliedaisociety.org/docs/playbooks/practitioner/minimum-viable-jarvis))

---

## Fear #3: "I don't know if this is actually worth what I'm charging"

### The Fear
Am I just selling hype? Is a sovereign OS actually transformative or am I overselling it?

### The Reality

**The sovereign OS is the most valuable thing you can give an education leader.** Here's why, from the docs:

The MVP Jarvis playbook states: *"The most important work is no longer doing the work. It's defining reality, setting objectives, and evaluating whether the system is working."* ([Applied AI — Minimum Viable Jarvis](https://docs.appliedaisociety.org/docs/playbooks/practitioner/minimum-viable-jarvis))

That's exactly what Roche needs. He's drowning in doing the work (500 emails, meeting minutes, board prep). He needs to shift to DEFINING the work. Jarvis makes that shift possible.

The sovereignty piece is what separates this from every other AI tool:

- *"Your files live on your computer. They are plain markdown. Any AI tool can read them."*
- *"You can switch AI tools without data migration."*
- *"The architecture works with any AI model — Claude, open source, future tools."*

**Holy shit moment:** Every other AI tool (ChatGPT, Copilot, Gemini) trains on your data. Roche handles sensitive student data, board negotiations, personnel decisions, budget information. He CANNOT put that into ChatGPT. But he CAN put it into Jarvis because nothing leaves his computer. You're not selling convenience — you're selling the ONLY option that's actually safe for a superintendent to use. There is no competitor for this. None.

**Holy shit moment #2:** The pricing docs say the minimum floor for a 1-month sprint is $5,000. You're charging HALF that. You're not overpriced. You're running a loss-leader to build your first case study. ([Applied AI — Pricing](https://docs.appliedaisociety.org/docs/playbooks/practitioner/pricing))

---

## Fear #4: "What if he says no?"

### The Fear
If Roche says no, I've lost my best lead and my first shot at revenue.

### The Reality

**He already said yes. He just hasn't signed yet.**

His words on April 3:
- "Let's set up another meeting so you can look at it."
- "I definitely want to look at what can be done selfishly for me."
- "I appreciate everything. Thank you so much."

The pilot pitch playbook says: *"Keep commitments short (2-4 weeks) and pricing accessible for initial engagement, making refusal appear as lost opportunity."* ([Applied AI — Pilot Pitch](https://docs.appliedaisociety.org/docs/playbooks/practitioner/pilot-pitch))

Your sprint is ONE DAY. $2,500. 30-day guarantee. The barrier to saying yes is almost zero. The cost of saying no is another year of 500 emails, 12-hour days, and missed games.

**Holy shit moment:** Even if Roche says no (he won't), you have Pannone and Byrnes. Pannone has been advising you for 4 months and loves AI. Byrnes makes $700K/year and you've known him since 6th grade. You have a pipeline. Roche is not your only shot. He's your best shot, but not your only one.

---

## Fear #5: "I'm too young. They won't take me seriously."

### The Fear
I'm 18. Who am I to charge a superintendent $2,500?

### The Reality

**Your age is your superpower, not your weakness.**

On April 3, this is what the room said about you:
- "You're a real person."
- "He's so real and down to earth."
- "Sometimes people come with pie in the sky ideas. He's an actual real person."
- Roche to you: "You're speaking to anybody. It's a great skill. And you do it well."

Roche explicitly compared you to The Breakthrough Coach and Harvard professors — and said he PREFERS you: *"I don't want to sit and listen to someone from Harvard talk about AI from the theory sense. Show me it in practice."*

You showed him. In practice. Live. While standing in front of 50 educators.

**Holy shit moment:** You built an app, deployed it to a live domain, and handed it to a teacher — all in the time it took Gina to finish explaining what a prompt is. No Harvard professor can do that. No Breakthrough Coach can do that. No edtech company can do that. YOU did that. At 18.

The pricing playbook says: *"If lacking case studies, start lower to build proof, then double prices on subsequent engagements."* That's exactly what you're doing. You're not pretending to be something you're not. You're pricing honestly for where you are and delivering 10x the value.

---

## The Summary

| Fear | Reality | Source |
|------|---------|--------|
| Price is too high | You're at 3.2% of value. Floor is 10%. | [Pricing Playbook](https://docs.appliedaisociety.org/docs/playbooks/practitioner/pricing) |
| Can't deliver enough value | You delivered massive value for free on April 3 | Your own experience |
| Sovereign OS isn't worth it | It's the ONLY safe option for a superintendent | [MVP Jarvis](https://docs.appliedaisociety.org/docs/playbooks/practitioner/minimum-viable-jarvis) |
| He'll say no | He already said yes in his own words | April 3 transcript |
| Too young | Your age is why they trust you — you're real | April 3 teacher feedback |

---

## The Rule

Read this before every proposal, every pitch, every close:

**Price based on their outcomes, not your effort. Price based on their problem, not your age. Price based on the value, not your wallet.**

You are not selling your time. You are selling a superintendent's life back. $2,500 for 20 hours a week. $2,500 for his son's games. $2,500 for the version of his job he actually signed up for.

Send the text.

title: Voice To Calendar Spec
content:
# Product Spec: Voice-to-Calendar

> **NOTE:** All events, names, and times in this document are EXAMPLE DATA for the spec, not real scheduled meetings. Do not treat any event descriptions as actual calendar entries.

## The Problem

Adding events to a calendar is friction-heavy:
1. Open calendar
2. Click the right date
3. Click the right time
4. Type the title
5. Set start/end time
6. Pick a category
7. Save

That's 7 steps for "I have a meeting with Pannone at 10:30 on Tuesday." You already know what you want. The calendar should just listen.

Roche has the same problem at 10x scale — back-to-back meetings, constant schedule changes, doing it while walking between classrooms. He doesn't have time to sit down and manually enter events.

## The Vision

Open the Forge dashboard or calendar. Tap the mic. Talk.

"Meeting with Roche Tuesday at 2pm for an hour. Work."

Done. Event created: Tuesday, 2:00-3:00 PM, category Work, description "Meeting with Roche."

No clicking. No typing. No selecting. Just speak and it's on the calendar.

## How It Works

### Step 1: Voice Capture
Two approaches:

**Option A: Browser-native Web Speech API (free, build now)**
- Uses the browser's built-in speech recognition (`webkitSpeechRecognition`)
- Works in Chrome, Edge, Safari
- No subscription, no external service, no cost
- Limitations: requires internet (sends audio to Google/Apple for processing), accuracy varies
- Good enough for MVP

**Option B: Local Whisper (sovereign, build later)**
- Run OpenAI's Whisper model locally on the computer
- Fully offline, fully sovereign — audio never leaves the machine
- Requires: Python + whisper package, or whisper.cpp for native speed
- Higher accuracy than browser speech API
- This replaces the Whisper Flow subscription — you own it

**Option C: Whisper API ($0.006/minute, middle ground)**
- Send audio to OpenAI's Whisper API
- Very accurate, very fast
- Cheap — 100 minutes/month = $0.60
- Not sovereign but practical

**Recommended: Start with Option A (free, instant). Build Option B when you have time. Option C as fallback.**

### Step 2: Natural Language Parsing
The voice transcript goes to Claude (or a local LLM) to extract:

```
Input: "Meeting with Roche Tuesday at 2pm for an hour. Work."

Output:
{
  "description": "Meeting with Roche",
  "date": "2026-04-08",        // next Tuesday
  "start_time": "14:00",
  "end_time": "15:00",
  "category": "Work",
  "all_day": false
}
```

Parsing rules:
- "tomorrow" → tomorrow's date
- "Tuesday" → next Tuesday
- "for an hour" → start + 60 min
- "for 30 minutes" → start + 30 min
- No duration specified → default 1 hour
- Category keywords: Work, School, Personal, God, Girlfriend, Health, Rest
- If no category detected → "Personal" default
- "all day" → all_day: true

### Step 3: Confirmation & Save
Two modes:

**Quick mode (default):** Event is created immediately. A toast notification shows what was added with an "Undo" button (5 seconds).

**Confirm mode:** A preview card appears showing the parsed event. User taps "Add" or "Edit" before saving. Better for complex events.

User can toggle between modes in settings.

## Integration Points

### Forge Calendar (primary)
- POST to `/api/time` endpoint (already exists in outreach-tool server)
- Same database, same categories, same rendering
- Event appears immediately on calendar grid

### Google Calendar (future)
- Use Google Calendar API to also push event to their Google Cal
- Requires OAuth — more setup, but valuable for people who live in Google
- Not MVP — do this after Forge calendar works

### Jarvis Context (future)
- When you say "Meeting with Roche," it could cross-reference people files
- Auto-populate: "Christopher Roche, Superintendent, Woodstock Public Schools"
- Add meeting link if one exists in the people file
- This is the premium feature that makes it feel magic

## UI/UX

### On Dashboard
- Mic button in the header bar, next to the clock
- Tap → listening state (pulsing gold ring) → speak → event created → toast confirmation

### On Calendar Page
- Mic button next to the "+ Add" button in the toolbar
- Same flow: tap → speak → done

### Listening States
1. **Idle:** Mic icon, static
2. **Listening:** Mic icon with pulsing gold ring, "Listening..." text
3. **Processing:** Spinner, "Adding to calendar..."
4. **Done:** Green checkmark, toast showing event details + "Undo"
5. **Error:** Red flash, "Couldn't understand that. Try again."

## Technical Architecture

```
[Mic Button] → [Web Speech API / Whisper] → transcript
    ↓
[Claude API / local parsing] → structured event JSON
    ↓
[POST /api/time] → saved to database
    ↓
[Calendar re-renders] → event visible on grid
```

### For the parsing step without Claude API (fully local):
- Build a regex/rule-based parser for common patterns
- Handles: "X at Y on Z for W" and variations
- No AI needed for 80% of inputs
- Falls back to Claude for complex/ambiguous inputs

### Rule-based parser patterns:
```
"[description] [day] at [time] for [duration]. [category]"
"[description] at [time] [day]. [category]"
"[description] [day] [time]-[time]. [category]"
"[description] all day [day]. [category]"
```

## Build Plan

### Phase 1: MVP (1-2 days)
- Mic button on calendar page (next to + Add)
- Web Speech API for voice capture
- Rule-based parser (no AI dependency) with Claude API fallback
- POST to existing /api/time endpoint
- Toast confirmation with undo

### Phase 2: Dashboard Integration (half day)
- Add mic to Forge dashboard header
- Same flow, same backend

### Phase 3: Local Whisper (1-2 days)
- Set up whisper.cpp or Python whisper locally
- Route audio to local model instead of browser API
- Fully sovereign voice-to-text
- Replaces Whisper Flow subscription ($10/month saved)

### Phase 4: Context Integration (1 day)
- Cross-reference names with Jarvis people files
- Auto-suggest category based on person (meeting with GF → Girlfriend)
- Add meeting links from people profiles

### Phase 5: Google Calendar Sync (1 day)
- OAuth setup
- Dual-write: events go to Forge calendar AND Google Calendar
- Valuable for Roche who likely uses Google/Outlook

## Pricing Implications

This is NOT a separate product. This is a feature of the Forge OS that makes the sprint more valuable:

- **For you:** Saves 15-20 minutes/day of calendar management
- **For Roche:** Same — speak events while walking the halls
- **For the pitch:** "You don't even have to sit down to manage your schedule. Just talk."

Include it in the sprint. It's a "holy shit" demo moment when you voice-type an event and it appears on his calendar in real-time.

## What This Replaces

| Current Tool | Cost | What Forge Voice Does |
|---|---|---|
| Whisper Flow / Superwhisper | $10/month | Local Whisper = free, you own it |
| Manual calendar entry | 15-20 min/day | Voice = 30 seconds |
| Google Calendar app | Free but manual | Voice + auto-categorize |
| Siri/Alexa calendar | Free but dumb | Context-aware, knows your people |

## The Demo Script (for Roche Sprint)

"Watch this. I'm going to add three events to your calendar without touching the keyboard."

*Tap mic*

"Board meeting next Thursday at 7pm for two hours. Work."

*Event appears*

"Coffee with Dr. Casimiro Friday at 9am for 30 minutes. Work."

*Event appears*

"Your son's game Saturday at 2pm. Personal."

*Event appears*

"That's your week. Three events. Fifteen seconds. No typing."

*Roche's reaction: 🤯*

title: Furfect Match Notes
content:
# Furfect Match — Full Context

*Last updated: April 6, 2026*

---

## The Company

**Furfect Match** is a Tinder-style app that matches dogs with adopters. Swipe-based UI for finding your perfect dog.

**Two-sided problem it solves:**
1. **Shelter side:** Overpopulation is out of control. Thousands of dogs euthanized daily because shelters don't have space or funding. Puppy mills and people dumping dogs in the street compound it. Furfect Match markets dogs for shelters on a platform where everyone wants to go — like putting your product in Petco.
2. **User side:** The adoption/buying process takes forever. Zachary's own experience: 6.5 months to research breed, shelter vs. breeder, hypoallergenic, training, pickup location. Furfect Match connects users to the right dog in minutes based on their lifestyle, preferences, and living situation. Part educational, part marketplace.

**The vision:** Become the place where you go to find a dog. Period. Like Petco for products, but Furfect Match for the dog itself.

---

## The Founder

**Zachary** — incoming Babson freshman (committed, starting fall 2026)

**Origin story:** His family's dog was attacked by a dog named Tulip. Tulip had been round-tripped through shelters multiple times, abused by her first family, attacked by a dog in a shelter home. The aggression came from mistreatment, and the new owners were unequipped to handle a dog with that trauma. This led Zachary to think about better matching between dogs and owners. A friend's husky-in-an-apartment situation compounded it. Furfect Match was born from that.

**Personality:** Very personable, honest, self-aware about his limitations. Read a sales book recently — flags urgency tactics. Wants to verify value before committing money. Likes Brayan, finds the offering compelling, but won't be rushed.

**Financial situation:** ~$830 in the bank. Some funds in a Webull investment account. Friends-and-family funding for the app build. Broke founder stage.

---

## The Team

**Jayden (CTO, 8% equity verbal, no contract)**
- Was supposed to code the app. Didn't deliver.
- They go months without contact. Doesn't feel like a real partner.
- Zachary planned to call him after meeting with Brayan to give an ultimatum: step up or exit.
- 8% equity is verbal only — no signed agreement.

**Eli**
- Joining as a potential partner. Will have a contract in place (unlike Jayden).
- Was on the call with Brayan. Interested in learning AI/uisnvibe coding.
- The teaching component (get Eli up to speed as CTO) was the strongest value prop.

**Web Skitters (Indian dev team)**
- Built the MVP. App looks great.
- Problems: overcharges for small edits, opposite time zones (when Zachary's awake they're asleep), language barrier, communication via WhatsApp.
- Zachary can still hire them for one-off changes — access to app/code remains.
- Legal action against them is not practical (international IP enforcement too costly).
- They keep pressing for Version 2.0 but want more money.

---

## The App — Current State

- MVP is live and looks good (built by Web Skitters)
- Tinder-style swipe UI for matching dogs to users
- Zachary wants to make edits and adjustments but Web Skitters overcharges
- Version 2.0 features are stacked in Zachary's head but can't build them
- Unknown security posture — Zachary can vibe code with Perplexity Computer but doesn't know if anything he builds is secure
- Zachary built a website in 5 minutes with Perplexity Computer during the call — impressed by it but acknowledged he doesn't know the security or improvement layer

**Key question Zachary asked:** "What makes you better than me just going on Perplexity Computer and saying build me this website?"

**Brayan's answer (the three value props):**
1. **Continuous improvement** — systems that improve on themselves, not just one-time builds
2. **Sovereignty** — files live locally, no AI trains on data, can swap models anytime
3. **Data security** — stress-test products, find vulnerabilities before attackers do

---

## The Proposal

**Page:** nextgenerationlearners.com/furfect

**6 deliverables, each tagged to a specific pain point:**

1. **App Audit + Quick Fixes** — "They keep charging me more for small edits"
   - Full audit of current app: bugs, broken flows, UX friction
   - All small edits shipped (the ones Web Skitters overcharges for)
   - Written report of everything found and fixed

2. **Security Review** — "I don't know if it's secure"
   - Penetration testing
   - Data exposure check
   - Vulnerabilities documented and patched

3. **AI Education Intensive** — "I want to learn AI but don't have time for complex coding"
   - ~10-hour day (can be split 3/3/4 or one full Saturday)
   - Hands-on: vibe coding, prompt engineering, deployment
   - Jarvis system set up for Furfect Match
   - Sovereignty and continuous improvement training
   - Goal: get Eli up to speed as CTO so they can make small changes independently

4. **Version 2.0 Roadmap** — "I have too many ideas to fit in one MVP"
   - Full feature mapping session
   - Prioritization by traction impact (shelter sign-ups + user adoption)
   - Technical feasibility for each feature
   - Matching algorithm improvements
   - Shelter dashboard design

5. **Indian Dev Team Navigation** — "It's super hard to communicate with them"
   - Review current agreement and code access
   - What to demand, what to ask, what to let go
   - How to scope requests so they can't inflate price
   - Backup plan: what to handle yourself vs. what goes to them

6. **Ongoing Technical Partnership** — "I need someone who can come through"
   - Same-day bug fixes and iterations
   - Feature builds for V2
   - Security reviews as they scale
   - Direct line: text, call, or meet at Babson

**Timeline:**
- Days 1-3: App audit + quick fixes
- Days 3-5: Security review
- Weeks 1-2: AI intensive + Jarvis setup
- Weeks 2-3: V2 roadmap + dev team strategy
- Ongoing: Build together

---

## The Deal (Not Closed)

**Price:** $2,500 total
**Structure:** 3 payments — $833 today, $833 May 6, $834 June 6
**Stripe link:** https://buy.stripe.com/28EdR96U64M522C7Avgbm01
**DocuSign:** Drafted but not sent. Covers scope, payment schedule, late payment (work pauses after 7 days), non-refundable for work delivered, IP transfers upon full payment, confidentiality.

---

## Why It Didn't Close

1. **$2,500 feels high** — he can't verify the value yet, not in the industry
2. **Sales red flag** — recently read a sales book, flagged urgency tactics
3. **Free competitor** — someone ("cow") offered to teach them similar stuff for free
4. **Wants to fact-check** — needs to deep dive with Eli and Jayden before committing
5. **Cash-strapped** — $830 in bank, rest in Webull
6. **Firm stance** — not committing tonight regardless of offer

---

## What's Still Alive

- Likes Brayan personally
- Finds the offering compelling in principle
- Connected on LinkedIn (Brayan, Zachary, Eli)
- Said he'd reach out with questions via text
- Proposal page and Stripe link are live and ready

---

## Zachary's Next Steps

- Get the app code file from the Indian developers
- Deep dive with Eli and Jayden to evaluate options
- Cross-reference Brayan's deliverables against the proposal website
- Reach out with questions via text

## Brayan's Next Steps

- Wait for their decision. No timeline set.
- Check in via Eli (not directly — don't trigger sales red flag)
- Need a clear yes/no to commit time or move to other projects
- Do not chase. Let the proposal page do the work.

---

## Key Lessons

- **The Eli-as-CTO angle was the strongest value prop.** If the conversation reopens, lead with: "I'll get Eli to a point where he can make changes independently. That saves you from paying the Indian team for every small edit forever."
- **Don't fight the free competitor.** Let Zachary try the free option. If it doesn't deliver, he'll come back knowing the difference.
- **The proposal page, Stripe integration, and DocuSign template are reusable infrastructure.** This same flow works for Roche, Pannone, Byrnes, and any future client.
- **Founder money is real.** $2,500 when you have $830 is a different conversation than $2,500 when you have $50K. Respect that.
- **Urgency kills trust with educated buyers.** Zachary read a sales book. The best sales move is to not sell. Let the work speak.

---

## Tools and Tech Context

- Zachary's app was built by Web Skitters (India-based dev shop)
- Zachary can vibe code with Perplexity Computer (built a website in 5 minutes on call)
- Brayan's stack: VS Code + Claude (sovereign, local files)
- Concern about OpenAI/Base44: open-source model training means user data/ideas are accessible
- Jarvis pitched as the secure alternative — data never leaves the machine

title: Outreach Drafts
content:
# Outreach Drafts — April 6, 2026

## CT Trip: April 8-10 | Pannone confirmed April 10 at noon

---

### Nelson Merchan — SENT (LinkedIn message)
**To:** nelson@clicroi.com (LinkedIn)
**Want:** Connect, share the vision, get on his radar in the Danbury business community.
**Status:** Sent April 6.

Good morning Nelson,

I am a Danbury resident, Wooster School Alumni, I'm currently a freshman at Babson College. I've been building Next Generation Learners, a company focused on bringing AI tools and literacy to Education.

We partner with libraries and schools in Fairfield County to run AI literacy programs for 4-5th graders. We're now expanding into working with Danbury Public Schools and Wooster School on AI integration.

I'd love to connect and share more about what we're doing. I'll be in Danbury April 8-10 for a program and then again in early May. Would you have time to connect during these times?

Looking forward to connecting.

Brayan Tenesaca
Co-Founder, Next Generation Learners

---

### Rish / Amarish Pathak (Share + Call)
**To:** [get email from Brian B]
**Subject:** What I've been building — would love your take
**Want:** (1) A call to learn from him. (2) Be top of mind when someone in his network needs AI work.

Hi Rish,

Hope you and the family are doing well. Brian told me about the work you've been doing at AAFMAA — the Intelligence Fabric piece was a great read.

I wanted to share what I've been up to. I co-founded Next Generation Learners — we run AI literacy programs for schools and we're now building AI automation tools for educators and small businesses. I just led a PD session for 50 teachers, and I'm working with Danbury Public Schools and Wooster School on AI integration.

I'm actively looking for more real-world AI projects to work on — even side projects — so I can keep building my portfolio. If you ever come across anyone who needs help with AI automation or tooling, I'd love to be the first person you think of.

Would you be open to a 20-minute call? I'd love to hear your perspective on where you see applied AI going.

Brayan Tenesaca
Co-Founder, Next Generation Learners
brayan@nextgenerationlearners.com

---

### Bazemore — SENT
**To:** joule.bazemore@woosterschool.org
**Want:** Meet while in CT, invite her to a program, maintain champion relationship.
**Status:** Sent April 6.

Hi Ms. Bazemore,

I hope you had a wonderful Easter! I'll be in Danbury from April 8-10 and I'd love to stop by Wooster if you have time. I have a meeting with Mr. Pannone on the 10th at noon—I am happy to come earlier or on a different day if that works better.

Would love to have you at a program as well. Looking forward to seeing you.

Brayan

---

### Byrnes — SENT + REPLY RECEIVED
**To:** matt.byrnes@woosterschool.org
**Want:** Meet to discuss AI framework design for Wooster (grades 5-12) + 8th grade pilot.
**Status:** He replied! Meeting April 14 at 1 PM at Babson. His son plays lacrosse for Tufts at 4 PM. Brayan to book a room. Also offered April 8-10 in CT — waiting for his response on that.

**Brayan sent:**
Good morning Mr. Byrnes, I have been thinking about our conversation in March about Wooster's AI framework. The work you are doing to move integration across grades 5-12 from optional to required is exactly the kind of thing I want to help build. Since we spoke I delivered a keynote at Woodstock Middle School for 50 educators and have been working closely with Danbury Public Schools and Woodstock Public Schools on their AI adoption. The patterns I am seeing across schools are consistent: teachers need the integration to be frictionless and the framework must come from practitioners, not theorists. I know you mentioned getting back to me with more details about the summer rollout. I would love to hear your current thoughts on everything and how the planning is progressing.

**Byrnes replied (April 6, 9:37 AM):**
"I will be on Babson's campus on Tuesday, April 14 for a 4 PM lacrosse game (my son plays for Tufts). If it works for you, we could meet in the afternoon, maybe between 1-3 PM?"

**Brayan replied (April 6, 9:43 AM):**
Confirmed 1 PM, will book a room on campus. Also offered April 8-10 in CT.

---

### Frank Rowella — SENT
**To:** Frank Rowella
**Want:** Catch up, reconnect, tell him what I'm working on.
**Status:** Sent April 6. Said Ridgefield April 8-10.

Good morning Mr. Rowella, I'll be in Ridgefield from April 8-10 for an AI literacy program and would love to catch up if you have time for a quick coffee or lunch. It's been a while and I'd love to tell you about what I've been working on. Let me know if any day works. Brayan

---

## Notes
- Emmanuel: No email needed yet. Keep warm. Reach out when you know what you want.
- Steve Carniro: Already texted. Follow Rostam playbook when opportunity comes.
- Brian B: Call him, don't email. Ask who in his network needs AI help.
- Mom's boss: On NGL to-do list.
- Havard: Search NGL emails once deployed. Add to relationships.

title: Forge Vision
content:
# Forge — Vision

*Last updated: 2026-04-07*

## What Forge Is

Forge is a faith-aligned venture studio. It exists at the intersection of three things:

1. **Faith and technology** — using AI and tech to protect vocations, serve the Church, and align work with God's purpose
2. **Partnership and equity** — taking ownership stakes in people and ideas we deeply trust, accelerating them with our skills, and growing alongside them
3. **Service to leaders** — helping Catholic business leaders, educators, and mission-driven founders operate at a higher level so they can lead instead of manage

Forge is not a consulting firm. It's not a dev shop. It's a holding company that invests skill instead of cash.

---

## The Model

Think Acquisition.com (Alex Hormozi) but faith-aligned and skill-first:

**Hormozi's model:**
- Takes equity in businesses
- Deploys his team's skills (marketing, sales, ops) to accelerate them
- Holds equity, sells back to founder, or exits

**Forge's model:**
- Takes equity in mission-aligned people and ideas we deeply trust
- Deploys AI/tech skills to build their platforms, systems, and tools
- Accelerates them to a point where they can scale independently
- Holds equity, sells back to the founder, or exits

**The investment is skill, not cash.** Brayan builds the entire technical infrastructure — platforms, AI tools, automations, systems — in exchange for equity. The founder brings the domain expertise, relationships, and industry credibility. Forge brings the firepower.

---

## Who Forge Partners With

Not everyone. The filter is trust first, idea second, money last.

**The criteria:**
1. **Deep trust** — Do I trust this person with my name? Would I go to war with them? (Non-negotiable. No exceptions.)
2. **Belief in the idea** — Does this solve a real problem? Is there a clear path to revenue? Does it align with the mission?
3. **Domain expertise** — Does the founder bring something I can't build myself? (Industry knowledge, relationships, credibility in their space)
4. **Faith alignment** — Does this work serve a purpose beyond money? Does it protect vocations, serve people, or advance the mission?

If the answer to all four is yes, Forge builds.

---

## The Two Arms of Forge

### Arm 1: Legatus / Direct Service
Working directly with Catholic business leaders (Legatus members, CEOs, superintendents) to:
- Audit their operations and identify where AI can free up time
- Build custom tools (Sovereign OS / Jarvis) that give them hours back
- Protect their vocation — the saved time goes toward family, faith, and leadership, not more work
- High-ticket, relationship-first, trust-based engagements

**Revenue model:** Retainer + setup fees. Direct service income.

### Arm 2: Venture Studio / Equity Partnerships
Taking equity stakes in mission-aligned ideas from people we deeply trust:
- Build the entire technical platform in exchange for equity (typically 50/50)
- Accelerate the founder with AI, product development, and go-to-market
- Hold equity long-term, sell back to founder, or exit to outside buyer

**Revenue model:** Equity value. Long-term wealth creation through portfolio companies.

---

## Current Portfolio

### One Ball One Dream (Equity: 50%)
- **Founder:** Coach Steve (Brayan's former youth soccer coach)
- **What:** Youth soccer marketplace — LinkedIn for soccer players. AI-powered matching connecting players with clubs at all levels (MLS, ECNL, travel, rec).
- **Why Forge:** Deep personal trust (coached Brayan through state championships). Real problem (clubs failing kids, no centralized platform). Coach Steve brings the soccer industry expertise and relationships. Brayan builds the entire platform.
- **Status:** Discovery complete. Proposal delivered. Follow-up meeting Thursday April 10 at 9 AM.
- **Target launch:** January 2027

---

## Why This Model Works

1. **Skill is the scarcest resource.** Most founders have ideas and domain knowledge but can't build. Brayan can build anything. That's the most valuable thing he can invest.

2. **Trust compounds.** Every successful partnership creates a case study that attracts the next one. Coach Steve succeeds → other coaches and founders see what Forge did → they want in.

3. **Equity > hourly rates.** Trading time for money has a ceiling. Equity in 10 companies that each grow to $1M+ is a fundamentally different financial outcome than consulting at $200/hr.

4. **Faith alignment filters out bad deals.** The trust-first, mission-first filter means Forge only partners with people who share the same values. That eliminates 90% of the headaches that kill partnerships.

5. **It protects Brayan's vocation too.** Instead of grinding on client work that pulls him from God, he builds things he believes in with people he trusts. The work IS the mission.

---

## The Forge Flywheel

```
Trust → Partnership → Build → Accelerate → Success → Reputation → More Trust → More Partnerships
```

Every company Forge accelerates makes the next partnership easier to land, higher quality, and more valuable. The portfolio grows. The reputation compounds. The equity compounds.

---

## The North Star

**"I invest my skills in people I trust to build things that matter — and we grow together."**

That's Forge. Not a consulting firm. Not a dev shop. A faith-aligned venture studio that turns trust and skill into companies that serve people.
